title: 10  Chapter 10 - Bayesian Model Comparison
text:
10.1 Learning Objectives
After completing this chapter, you should be able to:

title: 10  Chapter 10 - Bayesian Model Comparison
text:
Calculate and interpret Bayes factors
Understand Occams razor in the context of Bayesian inference
Compare models using evidence
Distinguish between Bayesian and Frequentist hypothesis testing
Apply model comparison to practical problems

title: 10  Chapter 10 - Bayesian Model Comparison
text:
10.2 Overview






This chapter covers material from the lecture slides: Advanced_Statistics_model_comparison.pdf

title: 10  Chapter 10 - Bayesian Model Comparison
text:
10.3 Bayes Factors

10.3.1 Comparing Hypotheses
The Bayes factor compares evidence for two hypotheses:
\[R = \frac{P(H_2 | D)}{P(H_1 | D)} = \frac{P(D | H_2)}{P(D | H_1)} \times \frac{P(H_2)}{P(H_1)}\]
The Bayes factor (BF) is the ratio of marginal likelihoods:
\[\text{BF}_{21} = \frac{P(D | H_2)}{P(D | H_1)}\]


10.3.2 Interpretation



Bayes Factor
Evidence for \(H_2\)




1-3
Not worth more than a bare mention


3-20
Positive


20-150
Strong


> 150
Very strong





10.3.3 Evidence (Marginal Likelihood)
\[P(D | H) = \int P(D | \theta, H) P(\theta | H) d\theta\]
The evidence: 1. Normalizes the posterior 2. Enables model comparison 3. Marginalizes over parameters

title: 10  Chapter 10 - Bayesian Model Comparison
text:
10.4 Occam’s Razor

10.4.1 The Principle
“Pluralitas non est ponenda sine necessitate” (Entities should not be multiplied without necessity).
In Bayesian terms: Models that are too complex are automatically penalized.


10.4.2 Laplace Approximation
Assuming the likelihood is peaked at \(\hat{\theta}\) with width \(\Delta\theta_{\text{eff}}\):
\[P(D | H) \approx \underbrace{P(D | \hat{\theta}, H)}_{\text{best fit}} \times \underbrace{\pi(\hat{\theta}) \Delta\theta_{\text{eff}}}_{\text{Occam factor}}\]
Key idea: Complex models spread prior mass over a large parameter space, so only a small region fits the data.


10.4.3 Toy Example: Sequence Prediction
Consider the sequence \(D = (-1, 3, 7, 11)\). What comes next?
Two hypotheses: - \(H_1\): Linear (arithmetic progression), 1 parameter - \(H_2\): Cubic, 3 parameters
Both fit the data perfectly! But which is better?

# Generate the two predictions
n <- 1:6

# H1: Linear (y_{n+1} = y_n + 4)
y_H1 <- -1 + 4*(n - 1)

# H2: Cubic (complex recurrence)
y_H2 <- c(-1, 3, 7, 11, -19.9, 1043.8)

# Plot
plot(1:4, y_H2[1:4], pch = 16, xlim = c(1, 6), ylim = c(-20, 50),
     xlab = "n", ylab = "y",
     main = "Model Comparison: Linear vs Cubic")
points(5:6, y_H2[5:6], pch = 17, col = "red")
lines(n, y_H1, col = "blue", lty = 2)
legend("topleft", legend = c("Data", "H2 predictions", "H1 (linear)"),
       pch = c(16, 17, NA), col = c("black", "red", "blue"),
       lty = c(NA, NA, 2))








The cubic model (\(H_2\)) makes wild predictions, while the linear model (\(H_1\)) gives sensible extrapolation.

title: 10  Chapter 10 - Bayesian Model Comparison
text:
10.5 Model Comparison

10.5.1 Comparing Models with Equal Priors
If \(P(H_1) = P(H_2)\):
\[R = \text{BF} = \frac{P(D | H_2)}{P(D | H_1)}\]


10.5.2 Occam Factor
When both models fit equally well at their best parameters:
\[\text{BF} \approx \frac{\pi_2(\hat{\theta}_2) \Delta\theta_2}{\pi_1(\hat{\theta}_1) \Delta\theta_1} = \frac{\text{Occam}_2}{\text{Occam}_1}\]
The Occam factor = fraction of parameter space that fits the data.


10.5.3 Estimating Occam Factors
For uniform priors over parameter ranges of size \(L\):
\[\text{Occam} \approx \frac{\Delta\theta}{L}\]
For a polynomial model with parameters \(\theta_k\): - \(\Delta\theta_k \sim \frac{\sigma}{n^k}\) - Higher-order parameters must be finely tuned


10.5.4 Example Calculation
Simple model (\(H_1\), 1 parameter): \[\Delta d \sim \frac{\sigma}{N} \Rightarrow \text{Occam}_1 \sim \frac{\sigma}{NL}\]
Cubic model (\(H_2\), 3 parameters): \[\Delta\alpha \sim \frac{\sigma}{N^3}, \Delta\beta \sim \frac{\sigma}{N^2}, \Delta\gamma \sim \frac{\sigma}{N}\] \[\text{Occam}_2 \sim \frac{\sigma^3}{L^3 N^6}\]
Ratio: \[R \sim \frac{\text{Occam}_2}{\text{Occam}_1} \sim \frac{\sigma^2}{L^2 N^5}\]
For typical values, this \(\ll 1\), strongly favoring the simpler model.

title: 10  Chapter 10 - Bayesian Model Comparison
text:
10.6 Physical Interpretation

10.6.1 Simple Model (\(H_1\)):

Many parameter values give similar predictions
Stable and predictive
Robust to perturbations



10.6.2 Complex Model (\(H_2\)):

Requires fine-tuning of parameters
Fits data as a coincidence
Most of parameter space doesnt explain the data

Conclusion: Complex models are penalized because most of their parameter space doesnt explain the data.

title: 10  Chapter 10 - Bayesian Model Comparison
text:
10.7 Frequentist Hypothesis Testing

10.7.1 Likelihood Ratio Test
Model: \((\Omega, \mathcal{F}, \mathbb{P}_\theta)\), \(\theta \in \Theta\)
Hypotheses: - \(H_0: \theta \in \Theta_0\) - \(H_1: \theta \in \Theta_1\)
Test Statistic: \[\Lambda(X) = \frac{\text{best fit under } H_0}{\text{best fit overall}} = \frac{\sup_{\theta \in \Theta_0} \mathbb{P}_\theta(X)}{\sup_{\theta \in \Theta} \mathbb{P}_\theta(X)}\]
Interpretation: - \(\Lambda \approx 1\): Restricted model explains as well as full one - \(\Lambda \ll 1\): Full model explains much better (evidence against \(H_0\))


10.7.2 p-values
Log-likelihood difference: \[T(X) = -2\ln\Lambda(X) = 2(\ln L_{\text{full}} - \ln L_{H_0})\]
Under \(H_0\), \(T(X) \sim \chi^2_k\) where \(k\) = difference in dimensions.
p-value: \[p(X_{\text{obs}}) = \sup_{\theta \in \Theta_0} \mathbb{P}_\theta(T(X) \geq T(X_{\text{obs}}))\]
Decision: Reject \(H_0\) if \(p < \alpha\).


10.7.3 Key Differences with Bayesian Approach



Aspect
Frequentist
Bayesian




Probability of hypotheses
Not defined
Well-defined


Evidence
p-values, likelihood ratio
Bayes factor


Complexity
Manual (AIC, BIC)
Automatic (Occam factor)


Decision
Binary (reject/fail to reject)
Probabilistic

title: 10  Chapter 10 - Bayesian Model Comparison
text:
10.8 Bayesian vs Frequentist

10.8.1 Bayesian:

Assign probabilities to hypotheses
Use Bayes factor
Automatically penalizes complexity (Occam)
Requires specification of prior



10.8.2 Frequentist:

No probability for hypotheses
Use p-values and tests
Focus on error control (Type I, Type II)
Complexity penalties added manually

title: 10  Chapter 10 - Bayesian Model Comparison
text:
10.9 Generative Models

10.9.1 Definition
A generative model predicts observable data from parameters.
Simplest example: Linear model \[f(x; a, b) = a + bx\]
Measurements include noise: \[y = f(x; \theta) + \varepsilon\]
where: - \(\theta_f\): model parameters - \(\varepsilon\): measurement noise (often \(\varepsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)\))


10.9.2 Gaussian Measurement Model
\[P(y | \theta, H) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\left[-\frac{(y - f(x; a, b))^2}{2\sigma^2}\right]\]
Interpretation: - Measurements fluctuate around model prediction - Width controlled by \(\sigma\)


10.9.3 Likelihood for Dataset
Assuming independent measurements:
\[P(D | \theta, H) = \prod_j P(y_j | x_j, \theta, H)\]
For Gaussian noise:
\[P(D | \theta, H) \propto \exp\left[-\frac{1}{2\sigma^2} \sum_j (y_j - f(x_j; \theta))^2\right]\]
Connection to least squares: \[\chi^2 = \sum_j (y_j - f(x_j; \theta))^2\]


10.9.4 Evidence and Computational Aspects
The evidence (marginal likelihood): \[P(D | H) = \int P(D | \theta, H) P(\theta | H) d\theta\]
Roles: - Normalization of posterior - Model comparison - Marginalization
Computational Challenge: - Analytical solutions only in simple cases - High-dimensional integrals - Complex likelihoods
Standard approach: Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC)

title: 10  Chapter 10 - Bayesian Model Comparison
text:
10.10 Summary of Key Results

Generative model + noise defines likelihood
Bayesian inference updates prior using data
Posterior contains full parameter information
Evidence enables normalization and model comparison
Numerical methods often required

title: 10  Chapter 10 - Bayesian Model Comparison
text:
10.11 Summary
Key takeaways from this chapter:

Bayes Factor: Ratio of marginal likelihoods, quantifies evidence
Occams Razor: Complex models are automatically penalized
Occam Factor: Fraction of prior mass consistent with data
Bayesian vs Frequentist: Different frameworks with different interpretations
Model Selection: Balance fit quality with model complexity

title: 10  Chapter 10 - Bayesian Model Comparison
text:
10.12 Exercises






Exercise 1



Two models both fit the data equally well at their best parameters. Model A has 1 parameter with Occam factor \(10^{-3}\). Model B has 3 parameters with Occam factor \(10^{-9}\) for each parameter. Calculate the Bayes factor and determine which model is favored.








Exercise 2



Explain the statement “Fit quality is not enough. Model complexity matters.” Give a concrete example where a more complex model fits the training data better but generalizes worse.








Exercise 3



Compare the Bayesian approach to model selection (Bayes factors) with the frequentist approach (p-values, likelihood ratio test). What are the advantages and disadvantages of each?

text:

title: 11  Advanced Statistics for Physics Analysis: Bayesian Inference
section: 13.1 The basics: deductive logic and the Bayesian framework
text:
13.1 The basics: deductive logic and the Bayesian framework
Statistics is the discipline that concerns the collection, organization, analysis, interpretation, and presentation of data. The European Mathematical Society classifies statistics as a branch of applied mathematic 1, specifically a subclass of probability theory and stochastic processes. However, it is also seen as a distinct scientific discipline due to its unique focus on real-world uncertainty and data context, involving empirical data gathering and experimental design.
Probability theory is the mathematical foundation for statistics. It is concerned with the study of probability, that quantifies of how likely, or plausible, an event is to happen, or a proposition to be true. Why do we need to introduce probabilities or deal with statistics? Because any real-world phenomenon involves systems of which we can have only a partial knowledge. Therefore, situations we deal with in everyday life are not completely predictable: uncertainty cannot be eliminated, we need to model it. More specifically, we have to act under the following paradigm, the one of inductive logic: given that certain effects have been observed, what is (are) the underlying cause(s)? Intuitively, plausible reasoning should be based on adjusting our belief about something, based on the occurrence or non-occurrence of something. This contrasts with deductive logic: given a well defined cause, we can work out its consequences. It is used in pure mathematics or theoretical physics, in which we can derive many exact results starting from few axioms or fundamental deterministic laws plus initial conditions, respectively.
Bayesian probability is an interpretation of probability theory in which probabilities quantify degrees of rational belief about propositions, conditional on stated information. Formally, the probability
\[
P(A \mid I)
\]
represents the degree of belief that proposition \(A\) is true, given background information \(I\). Bayesian probabilities apply to arbitrary propositions, including hypotheses and models. Importantly, they are always conditional on information and are updated when new information becomes available. Belief updating, say about a certain hypothesis \(H\), is governed by Bayes’ theorem,
\[
P(H \mid D, I)=\frac{P(D \mid H, I) P(H \mid I)}{P(D \mid I)} \tag{1}
\]
which follows directly from the product rule of probability. Here \(D\) denotes data, which are the result of experiments. In this view, probability theory is not a theory of frequencies, but a calculus for consistent reasoning in the presence of uncertainty. It allows one to deal rigorously with problems like inference: we observe data \(D\), we wish to reason about hypotheses \(H\). Our conclusions are uncertain but must be logically consistent!
A framework for deductive reasoning under uncertainty must satisfy logical consistency, quantitative comparison of plausibility, coherent updating when new data arrive, and applicability to arbitrary propositions and models. Bayesian probability satisfies all of these requirements. Moreover, under mild and explicit assumptions, it can be shown that

Any system that extends Boolean logic to uncertain propositions in a consistent, quantitative, and general way must be equivalent to Bayesian probability theory.

In this precise sense, Bayesian probability is not merely one possible method of inference, but the unique deductive logic for reasoning from data under uncertainty. Alternative frameworks (frequentist statistics, likelihood methods, belief functions) either avoid assigning probabilities to hypotheses, abandon full deductive consistency, or restrict the scope of admissible questions.
It is Cox’s theorem to provide the formal justification for Bayesian probability as a logic of inference. It originated from the desire to determine the most general mathematical rules for manipulating degrees of plausibility assigned to propositions, under requirements of rational consistency. It is based on the following simple assumptions: denoting \(w(A \mid B)\) the plausibility of proposition \(A\) given \(B\),

degrees of plausibility can be represented by real numbers;
if \(A\) is more plausible than \(B\), then \(w(A \mid I)>w(B \mid I)\) (ordering consistency);
if a conclusion can be reached by different chains of reasoning, the assigned plausibility must be the same (logical consistency);
the plausibility of the conjunction \(A B\) depends only on \(w(A \mid I)\) and \(w(B \mid A, I)\), and the plausibility of the negation \(\bar{A}\) (also called complement) depends only on the plausibility of the proposition itself.

From these assumptions, one can show that there exists a monotonic regraduation of \(w\) (a change of numerical scale) such that the regraduated plausibility \(P\) satisfies the sum and product rule, respectively,
\[
P(A \mid I)+P(\bar{A} \mid I)=1, \quad P(A, B \mid I)=P(A \mid I) P(B \mid A, I) \tag{2}
\]
together with the fact that certain truth is represented by \(P(A \mid B)=1\). Here, the vertical bar ‘\(\mid\)’ means ‘given’, so that all items to the right of this conditioning symbol are taken as being true, and the comma is read as the conjunction ‘and’.
Relations (2) are precisely the axioms of probability theory, from which Bayes’ theorem then follows as a corollary. Thus Bayesian probability is best understood as extended logic: ordinary deductive logic applied to situations where truth is not fully known.

title: 11  Advanced Statistics for Physics Analysis: Bayesian Inference
section: 13.2 Handwaving introduction to Bayesian inference tasks
text:
13.2 Handwaving introduction to Bayesian inference tasks
All inference problems can be framed as “inferring unknown objects (parameters, models, functions, structures, futures, or actions) from data under uncertainty”. Different problems differ mainly in (1) what is unknown (2) what question is being asked (3) what notion of optimality is used.
In statistics and data analysis, parameter estimation, model comparison and prediction, are the three most familiar inference tasks - they are not the only ones, though.

Parameter estimation: given some data \(D\), for a specific model \(H\), with parameters \(\theta\), infer their values.
Model comparison (aka hypothesis testing): given some data \(D\) and a set of models \(\left\{H_{j}\right\}\), find out which one is best supported by data.
Prediction: given a model \(H\), inferred from the data \(D\), predict new data at some new location (in the parameter space or time).

Within the Bayesian framework, that we will study, the two problems of parameter estimation and model comparison are formally the same: model comparison is basically estimation on a discrete parameter space.
The central idea is that we always use Bayes’ rule to get the distribution of an unknown random variable based on the observation of an other random variable:
\[
\underbrace{p(\theta \mid x)}_{\text {posterior }} \propto \underbrace{p(x \mid \theta)}_{\text {likelyhood }} \times \underbrace{p(\theta)}_{\text {prior }} \tag{3}
\]
In the jargon of inference, we get the posterior (prob. of the unknown given the data) in terms of the prior (any previous belief we have on the unknown) and the likelyhood (the prob. of observed data given the unknown). As we see, and very differently from the frequentist approach, we consider the unknowns, e.g. the model’s parameter, as random variable to which we are able to associate a certain distribution, i.e. the posterior. Equation (3) is going to be our central instrument, to be applied in different situations depending on the specificities of the inference problem, such as, whether \(x\) and \(\theta\) are continuous or discrete variables.
Later, if we desire to extract a single numerical estimate of the unknown random variables, i.e. a point estimate, there are different options, each satisfying different optimality conditions. We will see for example that the estimator may be defined by the max of the posterior (MAP), or its mean (LMS), inter alia.
crumbs: 11  Advanced Statistics for Physics Analysis: Bayesian Inference

title: 11  Advanced Statistics for Physics Analysis: Bayesian Inference
text:
13.3 The practical machinery of inference
In practice, Bayesian inference requires computing integrals of the form
\[
\mathbb{E}[f(\theta) \mid D] \equiv \int f(\theta) p(\theta \mid D) d \theta \tag{4}
\]
(that is a conditional expectation value), as well as marginal distributions, credible intervals, etc. These integrals are typically high-dimensional and analytically intractable. We will see that Monte Carlo methods provide a general numerical strategy to evaluate them. The Monte Carlo principle is based on the following idea: if \(\theta_{1}, \ldots, \theta_{N}\) are samples drawn from the posterior \(p(\theta \mid D)\), then the approximation
\[
\int f(\theta) p(\theta \mid D) d \theta \approx \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} f\left(\theta_{i}\right), \quad \text { as } N \rightarrow \infty \tag{5}
\]
Thus, Bayesian computation reduces to the problem of generating samples from the posterior distribution. In this sense, Monte Carlo methods are not optional numerical tools, but an integral part of modern Bayesian inference, especially when dealing with large dimensional parameter space. We will study some of the available methods.

title: 12  A Short Recap of Probability Theory
text:
12.1 Probability Spaces and Axioms
This section provides a concise review of the basic concepts of probability theory that will be used throughout these lecture notes on Bayesian inference.
A probability space is a triple \((\Omega, \mathcal{F}, \mathbb{P})\), where:
The probability measure satisfies the Kolmogorov axioms:
\[
\mathbb{P}\left(\bigcup_{i=1}^{\infty} A_{i}\right)=\sum_{i=1}^{\infty} \mathbb{P}\left(A_{i}\right)
\]
crumbs: 12  A Short Recap of Probability Theory

title: 12  A Short Recap of Probability Theory
crumbs: 12  A Short Recap of Probability Theory

title: 12  A Short Recap of Probability Theory
text:
12.2 Events and Set Operations
Events (elements of the \(\sigma\)-algebra \(\mathcal{F}\)) can be combined using standard set-theoretic operations, which correspond to logical operations. The properties of the associated probabilities follow from the Kolmogorov axioms.
Union (Logical OR). Given two events \(A, B\), the union
\[
A \cup B=\{\omega \in \Omega: \omega \in A \text { or } \omega \in B\}
\]
represents the event that at least one of the events \(A\) or \(B\) occurs.
The probability of the union is given by the inclusion-exclusion principle:
\[
\mathbb{P}(A \cup B)=\mathbb{P}(A)+\mathbb{P}(B)-\mathbb{P}(A \cap B)
\]
If \(A\) and \(B\) are disjoint (mutually exclusive), i.e. \(A \cap B=\varnothing\) is the empty set, then
\[
\mathbb{P}(A \cup B)=\mathbb{P}(A)+\mathbb{P}(B)
\]
Intersection (Logical AND). The intersection
\[
A \cap B=\{\omega \in \Omega: \omega \in A \text { and } \omega \in B\}
\]
corresponds to the event that both \(A\) and \(B\) occur. Its probability is directly related to conditional probability:
\[
\mathbb{P}(A \cap B)=\mathbb{P}(A \mid B) \mathbb{P}(B)=\mathbb{P}(B \mid A) \mathbb{P}(A) \tag{6}
\]
whenever the conditioning probabilities are well-defined. The conditional probability \(\mathbb{P}(B \mid A)\) is the probability of event \(B\) given that \(A\) is taken as true/occurred.
Complement (Negation). The complement (or negation) of an event \(A\) is
\[
\bar{A}=\Omega \backslash A,
\]
representing the event that \(A\) does not occur. By normalization,
\[
\mathbb{P}(\bar{A})=1-\mathbb{P}(A) .
\]
Set Difference. The difference between two events is
\[
A \backslash B=A \cap \bar{B},
\]
corresponding to the event that \(A\) occurs but \(B\) does not. Its probability is
\[
\mathbb{P}(A \backslash B)=\mathbb{P}(A)-\mathbb{P}(A \cap B) .
\]
De Morgan’s Laws. For any two events \(A\) and \(B\),
\[
\overline{(A \cup B)}=\bar{A} \cap \bar{B}, \quad \overline{(A \cap B)}=\bar{A} \cup \bar{B} .
\]
These identities are often useful when computing probabilities involving negations.
Monotonicity. If \(A \subseteq B\), then
\[
\mathbb{P}(A) \leq \mathbb{P}(B) .
\]
In particular, probabilities are bounded as
\[
0 \leq \mathbb{P}(A) \leq 1 .
\]
Partition of the Sample Space. A collection of events \(\left\{A_{i}\right\}_{i=1}^{n}\) is called a partition of \(\Omega\) if:

\(A_{i} \cap A_{j}=\varnothing\) for \(i \neq j\),
\(\bigcup_{i=1}^{n} A_{i}=\Omega\).

In this case,
\[
\sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbb{P}\left(A_{i}\right)=1 .
\]
Partitions play a central role in Bayesian inference, where they often represent mutually exclusive hypotheses or models. For \(n=2\), we go back to a single event and its complement.
crumbs: 12  A Short Recap of Probability Theory

title: 12  A Short Recap of Probability Theory
text:
12.3 Random Variables
A real-valued random variable \(X\) is a measurable function
\[
X: \Omega \rightarrow \mathbb{R} .
\]
Rather than working directly with outcomes \(\omega \in \Omega\), probability theory is typically formulated in terms of random variables and their distributions. A random variable thus assigns a real number to each outcome, i.e. to each result of an experiment. Basically, \(X\) tells you which quantity you measure in your experiment.
Note the important distinction: in the following the random variable \(X\) is a function, mapping an event to real numbers, while \(x\) is the particular numerical value that it can take (e.g., in one realization).
crumbs: 12  A Short Recap of Probability Theory

title: 12  A Short Recap of Probability Theory
text:
12.4 Probability Mass and Density Functions
If \(X\) is a discrete random variable taking values in a countable set \(\left\{x_{i}\right\}\), its probability mass function (pmf) is
\[
p_{X}\left(x_{i}\right)=\mathbb{P}\left(X=x_{i}\right), \quad \sum_{i} p_{X}\left(x_{i}\right)=1
\]
If \(X\) is a continuous random variable, it is characterized by a probability density function (pdf) \(f_{X}(x)\) such that
\[
\mathbb{P}(a \leq X \leq b)=\int_{a}^{b} f_{X}(x) d x, \quad \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f_{X}(x) d x=1
\]
Probabilities at a single point vanish in the continuous case:
\[
\mathbb{P}(X=x)=0
\]
it only makes sense to ask the probability that \(X\) follows within an (infinitesimal) interval.
crumbs: 12  A Short Recap of Probability Theory

title: 12  A Short Recap of Probability Theory
text:
12.5 Cumulative Distribution Function
The cumulative distribution function (CDF) of a random variable \(X\) is defined as
\[
F_{X}(x)=\mathbb{P}(X \leq x)
\]
It is non-decreasing, right-continuous, and satisfies
\[
\lim _{x \rightarrow-\infty} F_{X}(x)=0, \quad \lim _{x \rightarrow+\infty} F_{X}(x)=1
\]
If \(X\) admits a density \(f_{X}\), then
\[
F_{X}(x)=\int_{-\infty}^{x} f_{X}(t) d t, \quad f_{X}(x)=\frac{d}{d x} F_{X}(x)
\]
Instead, for the discrete random variable X with \(\operatorname{pmf} p_{X}\) the CDF reads
\[
F_{X}(x)=\sum_{y \leq x} P(X=y)=\sum_{i: x_{i} \leq x} p_{X}\left(x_{i}\right) . \tag{7}
\]
In this case the CDF is made up of jumps of value \(p_{X}\left(x_{i}\right)=F\left(x_{i}\right)-F\left(x_{i-1}\right)\).
crumbs: 12  A Short Recap of Probability Theory

title: 12  A Short Recap of Probability Theory
text:
12.6 Joint Distributions
Given two random variables \(X\) and \(Y\), their joint distribution associated to \(P(X \cap Y)\) is described by:

a joint \(\operatorname{pmf} p_{X, Y}(x, y)\) in the discrete case;
a joint pdf \(f_{X, Y}(x, y)\) in the continuous case.

Marginal distributions are obtained by summation or integration:
\[
p_{X}(x)=\sum_{y} p_{X, Y}(x, y), \quad f_{X}(x)=\int f_{X, Y}(x, y) d y
\]
crumbs: 12  A Short Recap of Probability Theory

title: 12  A Short Recap of Probability Theory
text:
12.7 Conditional Probability and Conditional Distributions
For random variables, the conditional distribution of \(X\) given \(Y=y\) is defined by
\[
p_{X \mid Y}(x \mid y)=\frac{p_{X, Y}(x, y)}{p_{Y}(y)}, \quad f_{X \mid Y}(x \mid y)=\frac{f_{X, Y}(x, y)}{f_{Y}(y)} \tag{8}
\]
whenever the denominator is non-zero. The joint distribution factorizes as
\[
p_{X, Y}(x, y)=p_{X \mid Y}(x \mid y) p_{Y}(y), \quad f_{X, Y}(x, y)=f_{X \mid Y}(x \mid y) f_{Y}(y)
\]
crumbs: 12  A Short Recap of Probability Theory

title: 12  A Short Recap of Probability Theory
text:
12.8 Expectation and Moments
The expectation (mean) of a random variable \(X\) is defined as
\[
\mathbb{E}[X]= \begin{cases}\sum_{x} x p_{X}(x), & \text { discrete case } \\ \int x f_{X}(x) d x, & \text { continuous case. }\end{cases}
\]
More generally, for any function \(g(X)\), the expectation reads
\[
\mathbb{E}[g(X)]=\sum_{x} g(x) p_{X}(x) \quad \text { or } \quad \mathbb{E}[g(X)]=\int g(x) f_{X}(x) d x
\]
The \(n\)-th raw moment (i.e., moment about zero) of \(X\) is
\[
\mu_{n}^{\prime} \equiv \mathbb{E}\left[X^{n}\right]
\]
The \(n\)-th central moment (i.e., moment about the mean) of \(X\) is
\[
\mu_{n} \equiv \mathbb{E}\left[(X-\mathbb{E}[X])^{n}\right]
\]
and provide clearer information about the distribution’s shape. Note that \(\mu_{1}=0\), while the variance is the \(n=2\) central moment, given by
\[
\mu_{2} \equiv \operatorname{Var}(X)=\mathbb{E}\left[(X-\mathbb{E}[X])^{2}\right]=\mathbb{E}\left[X^{2}\right]-(\mathbb{E}[X])^{2}
\]
The standard deviation is the \(\sqrt{\mu_{2}} \equiv \sigma\). Finally, it is customary to define the standardized moment of degree \(n\) as \(\mu_{n} / \sigma^{n}\) that is, the ratio of the \(n\)-th central moment to the \(n\)-th power of the standard deviation. It offers a scale invariant, dimensionless measure for characteristics of a distribution.
crumbs: 12  A Short Recap of Probability Theory

title: 12  A Short Recap of Probability Theory
text:
12.9 Moment and Cumulant Generating Functions
The moment generating function (MGF) of \(X\) is defined as
\[
M_{X}(t)=\mathbb{E}\left[e^{t X}\right]
\]
when it exists in a neighborhood of \(t=0\). Then derivatives of \(M_{X}(t)\) yields the \(n\)th central moment of \(X\):
\[
\left.\frac{d^{n} M_{X}(t)}{d t^{n}}\right|_{t=0}=\mu_{n}^{\prime} \tag{9}
\]
The cumulant generating function (CGF) is
\[
K_{X}(t)=\log M_{X}(t)
\]
The \(n\)-th cumulant \(\kappa_{n}\) is obtained by
\[
\kappa_{n}=\left.\frac{d^{n} K_{X}(t)}{d t^{n}}\right|_{t=0}
\]
The first two cumulants coincide with the mean and the variance:
\[
\kappa_{1}=\mu_{1}^{\prime}, \quad \kappa_{2}=\mu_{2}
\]
Cumulants play a central role in Bayesian inference and statistical mechanics, as characterization of fluctuations and correlations.
Note that MGF and CGF may not exist because the integral (sum) defining the expectation value need not converge absolutely. In this case, one can make the replacement \(t \mapsto i t\) defining the characteristic function
\[
\varphi_{X}(t)=\mathbb{E}\left[e^{i t X}\right]
\]
whose derivatives give the central moments, \(\left.\frac{d^{n} \varphi_{X}(t)}{d t^{n}}\right|_{t=0}=i^{n} \mu_{n}^{\prime}\).
crumbs: 12  A Short Recap of Probability Theory

title: 12  A Short Recap of Probability Theory
text:
12.10 An important comment
All probabilities are always conditional ones! If \(I\) is the background condition, related to information we have, the probability of an event \(A\) is of the form \(P(A \mid I)\). It makes no sense to talk about the probability of the truth of the statement \(A\) without being explicit about the conditions upon which the assignment of probability is based.
crumbs: 12  A Short Recap of Probability Theory

title: 12  A Short Recap of Probability Theory
text:
12.11 Bayes’ formula
We start from that from the definition of joint probability (6). The key observation is that the joint probability \(\mathbb{P}(A \cap B)\) is symmetric in \(A\) and \(B\). Therefore, provided that \(\mathbb{P}(A)>0\) and \(\mathbb{P}(B)>0\), we may also write
\[
\mathbb{P}(A \cap B)=\mathbb{P}(B \mid A) \mathbb{P}(A) \tag{10}
\]
Equating (6) and (10) yields
\[
\mathbb{P}(A \mid B) \mathbb{P}(B)=\mathbb{P}(B \mid A) \mathbb{P}(A)
\]
that combine together to give Bayes’ formula:
\[
\mathbb{P}(A \mid B)=\frac{\mathbb{P}(B \mid A) \mathbb{P}(A)}{\mathbb{P}(B)} \tag{11}
\]
A frequently used generalization arises when \(\left\{A_{i}\right\}_{i=1}^{n}\) is a partition of \(\Omega\), i.e. \(A_{i} \cap A_{j}=\varnothing\) for \(i \neq j\) and \(\bigcup_{i} A_{i}=\Omega\), with \(\mathbb{P}\left(A_{i}\right)>0\). Using countable additivity,
\[
\mathbb{P}(B)=\sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbb{P}\left(B \cap A_{i}\right)=\sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbb{P}\left(B \mid A_{i}\right) \mathbb{P}\left(A_{i}\right)
\]
and substituting this into 11 gives
\[
\mathbb{P}\left(A_{k} \mid B\right)=\frac{\mathbb{P}\left(B \mid A_{k}\right) \mathbb{P}\left(A_{k}\right)}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbb{P}\left(B \mid A_{i}\right) \mathbb{P}\left(A_{i}\right)} \tag{12}
\]
In data analysis and inference, \(A\) are parameters \(\theta\) that define the model (or the models \(H\) that we want to test) of the phenomenon, \(B\) are the data \(D\) (or \(X\)) that we get from the experiment. This form emphasizes the interpretation of Bayes’ formula as an update rule, where prior probabilities are revised in light of observed information, \(B\). Note that the normalization \(\mathbb{P}(B)\) is a constant independent of \(A_{k}\) but has been written in terms of the data \(B\) conditioned on the parameters/model.
If, given a likelihood function \(p(B \mid A)\), the posterior distribution \(p(A \mid B)\) is in the same probability distribution family as the prior probability distribution \(p(A)\), the prior and posterior are then called conjugate distributions with respect to that likelihood function and the prior is called a conjugate prior for the likelihood function \(p(B \mid A)\). A conjugate prior is an algebraic convenience, giving a closed-form expression for the posterior—numerical integration may be otherwise necessary.
Data inference is a slide down from the summit of Bayes’ formula.
crumbs: 12  A Short Recap of Probability Theory

title: 12  A Short Recap of Probability Theory
text: A \(\sigma\)-algebra is the mathematical structure that tells you which subsets of a sample space \(\Omega\) are allowed to be called events, i.e. which questions about the outcome are meaningful and can be assigned probabilities. An event is a yes/no question about the outcome (“Did the outcome fall in this set?” or “Did this condition occur?”). A \(\sigma\)-algebra is the collection of all such questions that you decide to be able to ask, and that remain meaningful when you combine them using logic (AND, OR, NOT). We mention it here, but we heuristically ignore it in the following. See Appendix A for more details.↩︎
crumbs: 12  A Short Recap of Probability Theory

title: 13  The Typical Animals Encountered in the Statistical Zoo
text:
13.1 Standard discrete distributions
This section reviews the most common probability distributions encountered in statistical inference. For each distribution we give the probability law, the main parameters, and a short interpretation. These distributions will reappear frequently as likelihoods, priors, or posteriors in Bayesian analysis.
It is useful to recall the meaning of Bernoulli trial, as it will appear many time in the following. It is a random experiment with exactly two possible outcomes, “success” and “failure”, in which the probability of success \(p\), thus of failure \(q=1-p\), are the same every time the experiment is conducted.
Discrete distributions describe random variables taking values in \(\mathbb{N}\) or finite subsets thereof.
crumbs: 13  The Typical Animals Encountered in the Statistical Zoo

title: 13  The Typical Animals Encountered in the Statistical Zoo
text:
13.1.1 Binomial (and multinomial) distribution
The binomial distribution gives the probability of observing \(x\) successes in \(n\) independent Bernoulli trials, each with success probability \(p\):






Implementation in R






# Probability mass function: P(X = k)
dbinom(3, size = 10, prob = 0.4)  # P(X=3) with n=10, p=0.4

[1] 0.2149908

# Cumulative distribution: P(X <= k)
pbinom(3, size = 10, prob = 0.4)  # P(X <= 3)

[1] 0.3822806

pbinom(3, size = 10, prob = 0.4, lower.tail = FALSE)  # P(X > 3)

[1] 0.6177194

# Quantile function
qbinom(0.95, size = 10, prob = 0.4)  # 95th percentile

[1] 7

# Random generation
rbinom(100, size = 10, prob = 0.4)

  [1] 6 7 4 6 5 3 4 1 4 4 6 6 2 1 5 5 3 4 4 2 3 2 3 4 7 1 3 5 6 5 4 2 5 6 5 4 5
 [38] 4 1 3 5 4 4 3 3 4 5 6 4 6 7 5 3 6 3 3 5 5 5 4 3 2 4 3 4 6 4 4 3 4 2 4 4 2
 [75] 3 5 1 4 3 3 2 5 7 5 5 4 3 3 2 3 5 5 4 2 5 4 4 3 7 7

# Visualization
x <- 0:10
y <- dbinom(x, size = 10, prob = 0.4)
barplot(y, names.arg = x, xlab = "k", ylab = "P(X=k)",
        main = "Binomial(10, 0.4)", col = "steelblue")









# Example: Restaurant Problem
# X ~ Binomial(n=4, p=8/30)
n <- 4
p <- 8/30
prob <- dbinom(2, size = n, prob = p)
cat("P(X = 2) =", prob, "\n")

P(X = 2) = 0.2294519 





\[
P(X=x)=\binom{n}{x} p^{x}(1-p)^{n-x}, \quad x=0,1, \ldots, n . \tag{13}
\]
The binomial coefficient \(\binom{n}{x}=\frac{n!}{x!(n-x)!}\) counts the ways to choose an unordered subset of \(k\) elements from a fixed set of \(n\) distinct elements.
Mean and variance are
\[
\mathbb{E}[X]=n p, \quad \sigma_{X}^{2}=n p(1-p)
\]
The case of a single trial \(n=1\) defines the Bernoulli distribution.






Example. Draws without replacement



In a restaurant 8 entrees of fish, 12 of beef and 10 of poultry are served. What is the probability that 2 of the 4 next customers order fish entrees?
There are \(12+8+20=30\) total entrees served. We assume that all dishes can be served an unlimited number of times. So the probability that a random customer orders fish is \(8 / 30=4 / 15\). Then, the number of fish orders among 4 customers is \(X \sim \operatorname{Binomial}(n=2, p=4 / 15)\). Therefore,
\[
P(X=2)=\frac{4!}{2!}\left(\frac{4}{15}\right)^{2}\left(1-\frac{4}{15}\right)^{4-2}=12\left(\frac{4}{15} \frac{11}{15}\right)^{2} \simeq 0.23 \tag{14}
\]


The multinomial distribution generalizes this setting to \(k\) possible outcomes with probabilities \(\left\{p_{i}\right\}_{i=1}^{k}\), describing the joint distribution of the counts \(\left(x_{1}, \ldots, x_{k}\right)\) with \(\sum_{i} x_{i}=n\). Its probability mass function reads






Implementation in R






# Generate multinomial samples
rmultinom(n = 1, size = 20, prob = c(0.2, 0.5, 0.3))

     [,1]
[1,]    7
[2,]    8
[3,]    5

# Example: 20 trials, 3 categories with probabilities 0.2, 0.5, 0.3
set.seed(123)
rmultinom(n = 5, size = 20, prob = c(0.2, 0.5, 0.3))

     [,1] [,2] [,3] [,4] [,5]
[1,]    3    3    7    4    4
[2,]    9    8   11    8   10
[3,]    8    9    2    8    6





\[
\begin{align*}
f\left(x_{1}, \ldots, x_{k} ; n, p_{1}, \ldots, p_{k}\right) & =\operatorname{Pr}\left(X_{1}=x_{1} \text { and } \ldots \text { and } X_{k}=x_{k}\right) \\
& = \begin{cases}\frac{n!}{x_{1}!\cdots x_{k}!} p_{1}^{x_{1}} \times \cdots \times p_{k}^{x_{k}}, & \text { when } \sum_{i=1}^{k} x_{i}=n \\ 0 & \text { otherwise, }\end{cases} \tag{15}
\end{align*}
\]


13.1.2 Geometric distribution
The geometric distribution gives the probability that \(n\) Bernoulli trials are needed to obtain the first success:






Implementation in R






# Note: R uses number of failures BEFORE first success!
# So P(N_fail = k) = (1-p)^k * p

# P(first success at trial 5)
dgeom(4, prob = 0.3)  # 4 failures before 1st success

[1] 0.07203

# Alternative: P(N = 5) directly
p_geom <- function(n, p) (1-p)^(n-1) * p
p_geom(5, 0.3)

# Mean and variance
mean_geom <- function(p) 1/p
var_geom <- function(p) (1-p)/p^2
cat("Geometric(p=0.3): Mean =", mean_geom(0.3), "Variance =", var_geom(0.3), "\n")

hist(rgeom(10000, prob = 0.3), breaks = 30,
     main = "Geometric Distribution (failures before success)",
     xlab = "Number of Failures")











\[
P(N=n)=(1-p)^{n-1} p \tag{16}
\]
It follows easily from the observation that the probability to succeed at the \(n\)-th trial is given by the probability of failing \(n-1\) times, \((1-p)^{n-1}\), times the probability of success, \(p\).
The geometric distribution has mean \(\mathbb{E}[N]=1 / p\) and its variance is \(\sigma^{2}=(1-p) / p^{2}\).
Additionally, it is memoryless,
\[
P(N>n+m \mid N>n)=P(N>m)
\]
meaning that the probability of waiting an additional \(m\) trials for success does not depend on how many trials have already occurred. To show that we compute the conditional probability
\[
P(N>n+m \mid N>n)=\frac{P(N>n+m)}{P(N>n)} \text {. }
\]
Since
\[
P(N>k)=\sum_{j=k+1}^{\infty}(1-p)^{j-1} p=(1-p)^{k}
\]
we obtain directly
\[
P(N>n+m \mid N>n)=\frac{(1-p)^{n+m}}{(1-p)^{n}}=(1-p)^{m}=P(N>m)
\]
We note that in the rare-event limit in which \(p \rightarrow 0\), we have that the variance diverges as \(\sigma^{2} \rightarrow 1 / p^{2}\). Namely, rare moments might happen at any moment, even though they have a negligible probability to happen at any moment.


13.1.3 Negative binomial distribution (or Pascal distribution)
The negative binomial distribution gives the probability of observing \(k\) failures before the \(r\)-th success in independent Bernoulli trials:






Implementation in R






# Plot negative binomial distributions
x <- 0:30
y1 <- dnbinom(x, size = 1, prob = 0.3)  # Geometric
y2 <- dnbinom(x, size = 5, prob = 0.3)

plot(x, y1, type = "h", ylim = c(0, max(y1, y2)),
     xlab = "k", ylab = "P(X=k)", main = "Negative Binomial")
lines(x + 0.15, y2, type = "h", col = "red")
legend("topright", legend = c("r=1 (Geometric)", "r=5"),
       col = c("black", "red"), lty = 1)







# Example: Expected number of failures before 3rd success with p=0.3
r <- 3
p <- 0.3
mean_nb <- r*(1-p)/p
cat("Mean failures before", r, "th success:", mean_nb, "\n")

Mean failures before 3 th success: 7 





\[
P(X=k)=\binom{k+r-1}{k}(1-p)^{k} p^{r}, \quad k=0,1,2, \ldots \tag{17}
\]
It generalizes the geometric distribution, which is recovered for \(r=1\).


13.1.4 Hypergeometric distribution
The hypergeometric distribution describes the probability of obtaining exactly \(k\) successes in \(n\) draws without replacement from a finite population of size \(N\) containing \(K\) successes:






Implementation in R






# Example: Urn with 40 white and 60 black balls (100 total)
# Draw 20 balls. Probability that at least 50 black balls remain?
# (i.e., at most 10 black balls drawn)

N <- 100    # Total balls
K <- 60     # Black balls (successes)
n <- 20     # Draws

# P(X <= 10) where X ~ Hypergeometric(N=100, K=60, n=20)
prob <- phyper(10, m = K, n = N - K, k = n)
cat("P(at most 10 black balls drawn) =", prob, "\n")

P(at most 10 black balls drawn) = 0.22098 

# Compare to binomial approximation
pbinom(10, size = 20, prob = 0.6)  # Approximation

[1] 0.2446628





\[
P(X=k)=\frac{\binom{K}{k}\binom{N-K}{n-k}}{\binom{N}{n}} \tag{18}
\]
This expression follows from counting the number of ways of choosing \(k\) successful items among the \(K\) available and \(n-k\) unsuccessful items among the remaining \(N-K\), divided by the total number of possible samples of size \(n\).
The hypergeometric distribution has mean
\[
\mathbb{E}[X]=n \frac{K}{N}, \tag{19}
\]
and variance
\[
\sigma^{2}=n \frac{K}{N}\left(1-\frac{K}{N}\right) \frac{N-n}{N-1} . \tag{20}
\]
In practice, the hypergeometric distribution generalizes Bernoulli trials to sampling without replacement, where the success probability is history-dependent. This is why it is expressed combinatorially rather than in terms of a fixed \(p\).






Example. Two-color urn problem



An urn contains 40 white balls and 60 black balls. If you draw 20 balls at random without replacement, what is the probability that at list 50 black balls remain in the urn at the end?
The total number of balls is \(N=100\). Let \(X\) be the random number of black balls drawn in the \(n=20\) draws. Since we want at least 50 black balls remaining in the urn, \(60-X \geq 50\), that is \(X \leq 10\). Since the draws are without replacement, \(X\) follows a hypergeometric distribution
\[
P(X=k)=\frac{\binom{60}{k}\binom{40}{20-k}}{\binom{100}{20}} \tag{21}
\]
Therefore, the required probability is
\[
P(X \leq 10)=\sum_{k=0}^{10} \frac{\binom{60}{k}\binom{40}{20-k}}{\binom{100}{20}} \simeq 0.22 \tag{22}
\]




13.1.5 Poisson distribution
The Poisson distribution describes the number of events occurring in a fixed interval when events happen independently at constant rate \(\lambda\):






Implementation in R






# Probability mass function
dpois(3, lambda = 2)  # P(X=3) with λ=2

[1] 0.180447

# Cumulative distribution
ppois(3, lambda = 2)  # P(X <= 3)

[1] 0.8571235

# Quantile
qpois(0.95, lambda = 2)  # 95th percentile

[1] 5

# Random generation
rpois(100, lambda = 2)

  [1] 4 1 6 0 4 1 1 2 2 1 3 3 6 1 0 3 3 4 3 5 4 4 6 5 2 4 1 2 1 1 1 3 0 2 0 4 1
 [38] 1 1 1 1 2 2 2 0 2 1 4 1 1 5 1 2 1 3 3 1 6 2 2 0 3 5 2 3 2 0 2 4 4 4 3 2 1
 [75] 0 1 2 1 2 3 5 0 2 3 0 1 2 2 3 2 3 3 4 2 2 2 3 3 3 4

# Visualization
x <- 0:15
y1 <- dpois(x, lambda = 2)
y2 <- dpois(x, lambda = 5)
y3 <- dpois(x, lambda = 10)

plot(x, y1, type = "h", ylim = c(0, 0.35),
     xlab = "k", ylab = "P(X=k)", main = "Poisson Distributions")
lines(x + 0.15, y2, type = "h", col = "blue")
lines(x + 0.3, y3, type = "h", col = "red")
legend("topright", legend = c("λ=2", "λ=5", "λ=10"),
       col = c("black", "blue", "red"), lty = 1)









# Demonstration: Binomial approaches Poisson
n <- 100
p <- 0.05
lambda <- n * p

x <- 0:20
binom_probs <- dbinom(x, size = n, prob = p)
pois_probs <- dpois(x, lambda = lambda)

plot(x, binom_probs, type = "h", col = "blue", lwd = 2,
     xlab = "k", ylab = "Probability",
     main = paste("Binomial(n=", n, ", p=", p,") vs Poisson(λ=", lambda, ")"))
lines(x + 0.15, pois_probs, type = "h", col = "red", lwd = 2)
legend("topright", legend = c("Binomial", "Poisson"),
       col = c("blue", "red"), lty = 1)











\[
P(X=x)=\frac{\lambda^{x}}{x!} e^{-\lambda}, \quad x=0,1,2, \ldots \tag{23}
\]
Both mean and variance are equal to \(\lambda\).
The Poisson distribution arises as a limit of the binomial distribution for rare events. We take the rare-event limit of (13), i.e. trials are many and success probability is small. Namely, we define the limit
\[
n \rightarrow \infty, \quad p_{n} \rightarrow 0, \quad n p_{n} \rightarrow \lambda>0
\]
where \(\lambda\) is kept fixed. For fixed \(k\) and large \(n\), we rewrite the binomial coefficient as
\[
\binom{n}{k}=\frac{n(n-1) \cdots(n-k+1)}{k!}=\frac{n^{k}}{k!}\left(1+\mathcal{O}\left(\frac{1}{n}\right)\right)
\]
Using \(p_{n}=\lambda / n\), we obtain
\[
\binom{n}{k} p_{n}^{k}=\frac{n^{k}}{k!}\left(\frac{\lambda}{n}\right)^{k}\left(1+\mathcal{O}\left(\frac{1}{n}\right)\right)=\frac{\lambda^{k}}{k!}\left(1+\mathcal{O}\left(\frac{1}{n}\right)\right)
\]
Moreover,
\[
\left(1-p_{n}\right)^{n-k}=\left(1-p_{n}\right)^{n}\left(1-p_{n}\right)^{-k} \underset{n \rightarrow \infty}{\longrightarrow} e^{-\lambda}
\]
since \((1-\lambda / n)^{n} \rightarrow e^{-\lambda}\) and \(\left(1-p_{n}\right)^{-k} \rightarrow 1\) for fixed \(k\). Putting everything together, we find
\[
\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} P\left(X_{n}=k\right)=\frac{\lambda^{k}}{k!} e^{-\lambda}, \quad k=0,1,2, \ldots
\]
Thus, in the rare-event limit the binomial distribution converges to the Poisson distribution with parameter \(\lambda\).

title: 13  The Typical Animals Encountered in the Statistical Zoo
text:
13.2 Standard continuous distributions
Continuous distributions describe random variables taking values in \(\mathbb{R}\) or subsets thereof and are characterized by probability density functions (pdfs).

13.2.1 Uniform distribution
The uniform distribution on an interval \([a, b]\) has constant density






Implementation in R






# Density
x <- seq(0, 1, length.out = 100)
y <- dunif(x, min = 0, max = 1)
plot(x, y, type = "l", main = "Uniform(0,1) PDF")







# Cumulative distribution
punif(0.5, min = 0, max = 1)  # P(X <= 0.5) = 0.5

[1] 0.5

# Quantile function
qunif(0.95, min = 0, max = 1)  # 0.95 quantile

[1] 0.95

# Random generation
samples <- runif(1000, min = -1, max = 1)
hist(samples, breaks = 30, probability = TRUE,
     main = "Histogram of Uniform(-1,1) samples")
lines(density(samples), col = "red")











\[
f(x)=\frac{1}{b-a}, \quad x \in[a, b] \tag{24}
\]
We denote such a uniformly distributed variable as \(X \sim \mathcal{U}(0,1)\). The expected value and the variance are, respectively,
\[
\mathbb{E}[X]=\frac{a+b}{2}, \quad \sigma_{X}^{2}=\frac{(b-a)^{2}}{12} \tag{25}
\]
It represents complete ignorance within fixed bounds and is often used as a simple prior.






Example. Sum of two independent, uniformly distributed variables



Take \(X_{1}\) and \(X_{2}\) independent random variables with uniform distribution in the interval \([0,1]\). Find the distribution of \(Y=X_{1}+X_{2}\).
First, we observed that the support of \(f_{Y}(y)\) will be in the interval \([0,2]\). By definition, we obtain the pdf of \(Y\) as
\[
\begin{align*}
f_{Y}(y) & =\int_{0}^{1} d x_{1} \int_{0}^{1} d x_{2} f_{X_{1}}\left(x_{1}\right) f_{X_{2}}\left(x_{2}\right) \delta\left(x_{1}+x_{2}-y\right) \\
& =\int_{0}^{1} d x_{1} \mathbf{1}_{0 \leq y-x_{1} \leq 1} \\
& =\int_{\max (0, y-1)}^{\min (1, y)} d x_{1} \\
& =\min (1, y)-\max (0, y-1) \tag{26-29}
\end{align*}
\]
where we used \(f_{X_{i}}\left(x_{i}\right)=1\). This is a triangular distribution with expected value \(\mathbb{E}[Y]=1\) and variance \(\sigma_{Y}^{2}=\int d y(y-1)^{2} f_{Y}(y)=1 / 6\). This is the special case \(n=2\) of the Irwin-Hall distribution which is a sum on \(n\) indipendent, identically distributed random variables in the the unit interval.




13.2.2 Normal distribution
The normal (Gaussian) distribution with mean \(\mu\) and variance \(\sigma^{2}\) has pdf






Implementation in R






# Standard normal
x <- seq(-4, 4, length.out = 200)
y <- dnorm(x, mean = 0, sd = 1)
plot(x, y, type = "l", main = "Standard Normal PDF",
     xlab = "x", ylab = "Density")

# Different parameters
y2 <- dnorm(x, mean = 1, sd = 2)
lines(x, y2, col = "red", lty = 2)
legend("topright", legend = c("N(0,1)", "N(1,4)"),
       col = c("black", "red"), lty = c(1, 2))







# Cumulative distribution
pnorm(0)              # P(Z <= 0) = 0.5

[1] 0.5

pnorm(1.96)           # ~0.975 (95th percentile)

[1] 0.9750021

1 - pnorm(1.96)       # Right tail probability

[1] 0.0249979

# Quantile function (critical values)
qnorm(0.025)          # -1.96 (lower 2.5%)

[1] -1.959964

qnorm(0.975)          # 1.96 (upper 2.5%)

[1] 1.959964

qnorm(0.95)           # 1.645 (one-sided 95%)

[1] 1.644854

# Random samples
samples <- rnorm(1000, mean = 100, sd = 15)
hist(samples, breaks = 30, probability = TRUE)
curve(dnorm(x, mean = 100, sd = 15), add = TRUE, col = "red")







# Standardization example
x_ex <- rnorm(100, mean = 50, sd = 10)
z <- (x_ex - mean(x_ex)) / sd(x_ex)  # Standardize
hist(z, probability = TRUE)
curve(dnorm(x), add = TRUE, col = "red")











\[
f(x)=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2 \pi \sigma^{2}}} \exp \left[-\frac{(x-\mu)^{2}}{2 \sigma^{2}}\right] \tag{30}
\]
We denote such a Gaussian distributed variable as \(X \sim \mathcal{N}\left(\mu, \sigma^{2}\right)\). If \(X_{i} \sim \mathcal{N}\left(\mu_{i}, \sigma_{i}^{2}\right)\) are \(n\) independent random variables, it holds true that any linear combination \(Y=\sum_{i=1}^{n} a_{n} X_{n}\) will be again a Gaussian distributed random variable with mean \(\sum_{i=1}^{n} a_{i} \mu_{i}\) and variance \(\sum_{i=1}^{n} a_{i}^{2} \sigma_{i}^{2}\)
The normal distribution plays a central role due to the central limit theorem (see later) and its analytical convenience in Bayesian inference.


13.2.3 Exponential distribution
The exponential distribution with rate \(\lambda>0\) has density






Implementation in R






# Density
x <- seq(0, 5, length.out = 100)
y1 <- dexp(x, rate = 1)
y2 <- dexp(x, rate = 0.5)
y3 <- dexp(x, rate = 2)

plot(x, y1, type = "l", ylab = "Density",
     main = "Exponential Distributions")
lines(x, y2, col = "red", lty = 2)
lines(x, y3, col = "blue", lty = 3)
legend("topright", legend = c("λ=1", "λ=0.5", "λ=2"),
       col = c("black", "red", "blue"), lty = 1:3)







# Mean and variance
lambda <- 2
mean_exp <- 1/lambda
var_exp <- 1/lambda^2
cat("Mean:", mean_exp, "Variance:", var_exp, "\n")

Mean: 0.5 Variance: 0.25 

# CDF: P(X <= x)
pexp(1, rate = 1)     # P(X <= 1) with λ=1

[1] 0.6321206

# Quantile
qexp(0.5, rate = 1)   # Median

[1] 0.6931472





\[
f(t)=\lambda e^{-\lambda t}, \quad t \geq 0 \tag{31}
\]
It describes waiting times between Poissonian events. Consider a Poisson process with constant rate \(\lambda>0\). Let \(N(t)\) be the number of events occurring in the time interval \([0, t]\). By definition of a Poisson process,
\[
\mathbb{P}(N(t)=k)=\frac{(\lambda t)^{k}}{k!} e^{-\lambda t}, \quad k=0,1,2, \ldots
\]
Let \(T\) be the waiting time until the first event occurs. The event \(\{T>t\}\) means that no events have occurred up to time \(t\), i.e. \(\{T>t\} \equiv\{N(t)=0\}\). Therefore,
\[
\mathbb{P}(T>t)=\mathbb{P}(N(t)=0)=e^{-\lambda t}
\]
The cumulative distribution function of \(T\) is then
\[
F_{T}(t)=\mathbb{P}(T \leq t)=1-e^{-\lambda t}, \quad t \geq 0
\]
Differentiating with respect to \(t\), we obtain the probability density function (31).
The exp distribution is memoryless, i.e.,
\[
P(T>t+s \mid T>t)=P(T>s), \quad \text { with } s, t \geq 0 \tag{32}
\]
To show that we compute
\[
\mathbb{P}(T>s+t \mid T>s)=\frac{\mathbb{P}(T>s+t \cap T>s)}{\mathbb{P}(T>s)}=\frac{\mathbb{P}(T>s+t)}{\mathbb{P}(T>s)} .
\]
Using the survival function of the exponential distribution,
\[
\mathbb{P}(T>u)=\int_{u}^{\infty} \lambda e^{-\lambda x} d x=e^{-\lambda u}
\]
we obtain
\[
\mathbb{P}(T>s+t \mid T>s)=\frac{e^{-\lambda(s+t)}}{e^{-\lambda s}}=e^{-\lambda t}
\]
which is (32).


13.2.4 Erlang distribution
The Erlang distribution is the distribution of the sum of \(n\) independent, identically distributed exponential random variables with rate \(\lambda\):






Implementation in R






# Erlang is a special case of Gamma with integer shape
t <- seq(0, 10, length.out = 100)
plot(t, dgamma(t, shape = 1, rate = 1), type = "l",
     ylab = "Density", main = "Erlang Distributions")
lines(t, dgamma(t, shape = 3, rate = 1), col = "blue")
lines(t, dgamma(t, shape = 5, rate = 1), col = "red")
legend("topright", legend = c("n=1 (Exp)", "n=3", "n=5"),
       col = c("black", "blue", "red"), lty = 1)







# Example: Waiting time until 3rd event
n <- 3
lambda <- 1
mean_erlang <- n/lambda
var_erlang <- n/lambda^2
cat("Erlang(n=3, λ=1): Mean =", mean_erlang, "Variance =", var_erlang, "\n")

Erlang(n=3, λ=1): Mean = 3 Variance = 3 





\[
f(t)=\frac{\lambda^{n} t^{n-1}}{(n-1)!} e^{-\lambda t}, \quad t \geq 0 \tag{33}
\]
It describes the waiting time until the \(n\)-th Poisson event.


13.2.5 Gamma distribution
The gamma distribution generalizes the Erlang distribution by extending \(n \mapsto \alpha\) to a positive real shape parameter:






Implementation in R






# Different parameterizations in R:
# shape = α, rate = λ (or scale = 1/λ)

x <- seq(0, 15, length.out = 200)
plot(x, dgamma(x, shape = 2, rate = 1), type = "l",
     ylab = "Density", main = "Gamma Distributions")
lines(x, dgamma(x, shape = 2, rate = 0.5), col = "red")
lines(x, dgamma(x, shape = 5, rate = 1), col = "blue")
legend("topright", legend = c("Γ(2,1)", "Γ(2,0.5)", "Γ(5,1)"),
       col = c("black", "red", "blue"), lty = 1)







# Conjugate prior: Gamma is conjugate prior for Poisson rate




\[
f(t)=\frac{\lambda^{\alpha}}{\Gamma(\alpha)} t^{\alpha-1} e^{-\lambda t}, \quad t \geq 0 \tag{34}
\]
We denote a Gamma distributed variable as \(X \sim \operatorname{Gamma}(\alpha, \lambda)\) with shape parameter \(\alpha\) and scale parameter \(\lambda\). In fact, if \(X \sim \operatorname{Gamma}(\alpha, \lambda)\) then \(c X \sim \operatorname{Gamma}(\alpha, \lambda / c)\) for any \(c>0\).
The mean and variance of the Gamma distribution (34) are, respectively,
\[
\mu=\alpha / \lambda, \quad \sigma^{2}=\alpha / \lambda^{2} \tag{35}
\]
The CDF of the Gamma distribution does not exist in explicit form.
The Gamma distribution is widely used as a conjugate prior for Poisson and exponential likelihoods. A special case is \(\alpha=k / 2\) and \(\gamma=1 / 2\), which yields the chi-squared distribution \(\chi_{k}^{2}\) representing the sum of squares of \(k\) independent standard normal random variables.


13.2.6 Beta distribution
The beta distribution on \([0,1]\) with parameters \(\alpha, \beta>0\) has density






Implementation in R






# Plot different beta distributions
x <- seq(0, 1, length.out = 200)
plot(x, dbeta(x, 2, 5), type = "l", ylim = c(0, 3),
     ylab = "Density", main = "Beta Distributions")
lines(x, dbeta(x, 5, 2), col = "red")
lines(x, dbeta(x, 2, 2), col = "blue")
lines(x, dbeta(x, 0.5, 0.5), col = "green")
legend("top", legend = c("Beta(2,5)", "Beta(5,2)", "Beta(2,2)", "Beta(0.5,0.5)"),
       col = c("black", "red", "blue", "green"), lty = 1)







# Conjugate prior: Beta is conjugate prior for Bernoulli/Binomial
# Posterior: Beta(α + k, β + n - k) after observing k successes in n trials


# Mixture of two betas
x <- seq(0, 1, length.out = 200)
mixture <- 0.5 * dbeta(x, 2, 5) + 0.5 * dbeta(x, 5, 2)
plot(x, mixture, type = "l", ylab = "Density",
     main = "Beta Mixture: 0.5 Beta(2,5) + 0.5 Beta(5,2)")







# Can model complex beliefs that single beta cannot




\[
f(x)=\frac{1}{B(\alpha, \beta)} x^{\alpha-1}(1-x)^{\beta-1} \tag{36}
\]
where the normalization constant is the Beta function
\[
B(\alpha, \beta)=\int_{0}^{1} t^{\alpha-1}(1-t)^{\beta-1} d t=\frac{\Gamma(\alpha) \Gamma(\beta)}{\Gamma(\alpha+\beta)} \tag{37}
\]
which is a symmetric function that can be written in terms of the Gamma function \(\Gamma(z)=\int_{0}^{\infty} t^{z-1} e^{-t} d t\). We denote a Beta distributed random variable as \(X \sim \operatorname{Beta}(\alpha, \beta)\). The mean and the variance of the Beta distribution (37) are, respectively,
\[
\mu=\frac{\alpha}{\alpha+\beta}, \quad \sigma^{2}=\frac{\alpha \beta}{(\alpha+\beta)^{2}(\alpha+\beta+1)} \tag{38}
\]
The pdf (37) in unimodal. A common choice to model bimodal distribution in the unit interval is by mixing two different Beta distributions, e.g., \(X \sim \frac{1}{2} \operatorname{Beta}\left(\alpha_{1}, \beta_{1}\right)+\frac{1}{2} \operatorname{Beta}\left(\alpha_{2}, \beta_{2}\right)\).
The Beta distribution is the natural conjugate prior for the Bernoulli and binomial distributions.

title: 13  The Typical Animals Encountered in the Statistical Zoo
text:
13.3 Limiting Theorems and Asymptotic Structure
Limiting theorems describe the behavior of sums of random variables in the large-sample limit and form a cornerstone of both classical and Bayesian statistics. They justify the emergence of deterministic behavior, Gaussian fluctuations, and non-Gaussian rare events from microscopic randomness. We review the Law of Large Numbers (LLN), the Central Limit Theorem (CLT), and the Large Deviation Principle, emphasizing their conceptual unity and their role in Bayesian inference. All in all, they form a hierarchy of asymptotic descriptions:

the LLN captures deterministic limits;
the CLT captures typical Gaussian fluctuations;
large deviations capture rare, non-Gaussian events.

Together, they provide a multiscale description of uncertainty that is fundamental to modern Bayesian inference and its asymptotic analysis.

13.3.1 Law of Large Numbers
Let \(\left\{X_{i}\right\}_{i=1}^{\infty}\) be a sequence of independent and identically distributed (i.i.d.) random variables. Define the sample mean






Implementation in R






# Demonstration of LLN
set.seed(123)
n <- 10000
x <- runif(n, -1, 1)  # Uniform on [-1,1], true mean = 0
running_mean <- cumsum(x) / (1:n)

plot(running_mean, type = "l", xlab = "n",
     ylab = "Sample Mean",
     main = "Law of Large Numbers")
abline(h = 0, col = "red", lty = 2)
legend("topright", legend = "True mean = 0", col = "red", lty = 2)











\[
\bar{X}_{n} \equiv \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} X_{i} \tag{39}
\]
There exist two different formulations of the LLN. They can be proved under the assumptions of finite mean \(\mathbb{E}\left[X_{1}\right]=\mu\) and variance \(\operatorname{Var}(X)=\sigma^{2}\) (or under milder conditions with some more work).
Weak law of large numbers. The sample mean converges in probability 1 to the expected value:
\[
\bar{X}_{n} \xrightarrow[n \rightarrow \infty]{\mathbb{P}} \mu \tag{41}
\]
Strong law of large numbers. The sample mean converges absolutely surely 2 to the expected value:
\[
\bar{X}_{n} \xrightarrow[n \rightarrow \infty]{\text { a.s. }} \mu . \tag{44}
\]
The law of large numbers states that averaging suppresses randomness: extensive quantities become deterministic in the large-sample limit. Fluctuations around the mean vanish relative to the size of the system.
Bayesian relevance. In Bayesian inference, the law of large numbers underlies:

posterior consistency, whereby accumulating data overwhelms the prior;
convergence of empirical averages to posterior expectations;
the interpretation of likelihoods as empirical averages of log-probability contributions.

In this sense, Bayesian updating can be viewed as a controlled application of the law of large numbers in probability space.


13.3.2 Central Limit Theorem
Let \(\left\{X_{i}\right\}\) be i.i.d. random variables with mean \(\mu\) and finite variance \(\sigma^{2}\). Then the pdf of (the scaled and shifted sum)






Implementation in R






# Demonstration of CLT with uniform distribution
set.seed(42)
n <- 50  # Sample size
m <- 1000  # Number of samples

# Generate sample means from uniform distribution
sample_means <- replicate(m, mean(runif(n, -1, 1)))

# Plot
hist(sample_means, breaks = 30, probability = TRUE,
     main = paste("CLT: Distribution of Sample Mean\nn =", n),
     xlab = "Sample Mean")

# Theoretical normal distribution
theoretical_mean <- 0
theoretical_sd <- sqrt((1/3)/n)  # Var(Uniform(-1,1)) = 1/3
curve(dnorm(x, theoretical_mean, theoretical_sd),
      add = TRUE, col = "red", lwd = 2)











\[
\frac{1}{\sqrt{n}} \sum_{i=1}^{n}\left(X_{i}-\mu\right) \tag{45}
\]
converges as \(n \rightarrow \infty\) to the normal distribution \(\mathcal{N}\left(0, \sigma^{2}\right)\). In order words, we can say that the sample mean \(\bar{X}_{n} \approx \mu+\sigma / \sqrt{n}\) as \(n \rightarrow \infty\).
CLT describes the universal emergence of Gaussian fluctuations around the deterministic limit prescribed by the law of large numbers. Remarkably, the limiting distribution depends only on the first two moments and is largely independent of the microscopic details of the original distribution.
Bayesian relevance. The CLT explains several ubiquitous Gaussian structures in Bayesian statistics:

asymptotic normality of posterior distributions (Bernstein-von Mises theorem);
Gaussian approximations to likelihoods and posteriors (Laplace’s method);
the prevalence of quadratic log-likelihoods and Fisher information as a local curvature.

From this viewpoint, Gaussian posteriors arise not as fundamental objects, but as effective descriptions of typical fluctuations in large-data limits.


13.3.3 Large Deviation Principle
While the CLT describes typical fluctuations of order \(n^{-1 / 2}\), it does not capture the behavior of rare, large fluctuations. This regime is described by large deviation theory. Let \(\left\{X_{i}\right\}\) be random variables and let \(\bar{X}_{n}\) denote their sample mean. We say that the sample mean satisfies a large deviation principle (LDP) 3:
\[
\mathbb{P}\left(\bar{X}_{n} \approx x\right) \asymp e^{-n \mathcal{I}(x)} \tag{46}
\]
where \(\mathcal{I}(x) \geq 0\) is the rate function, vanishing uniquely at \(x=\mu\). The symbol \(\asymp\) in (46) signifies scaled logarithmic equivalence, i.e. it is a short-hand for
\[
\mathcal{I}(x) \equiv-\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} \frac{1}{n} \ln \mathbb{P}\left(\bar{X}_{n} \approx x\right) \tag{47}
\]
LDP holds for \(\left\{X_{i}\right\}\) i.i.d random variables but also under milder conditions (e.g. ‘short range’ correlations between \(\left\{X_{i}\right\}\)).
The rate function \(I(x)\) quantifies the exponential cost of atypical outcomes. Expanding \(I(x)\) to second order around its minimum recovers the Gaussian fluctuations of the CLT, while the full function encodes strongly non-Gaussian tails. For \(n=\infty\), collapses to a Dirac delta functional centered on \(\mu\). For this reason, at large but finite \(n\) we say that the probability concentrates (around the LLN value).
Bayesian relevance. Large deviation principles play a central role in:

Bayesian consistency and posterior concentration rates;
the asymptotic form of marginal likelihoods and model evidence;
PAC-Bayesian bounds and information-theoretic generalization guarantees.

In Bayesian language, the posterior often concentrates as
\[
\pi\left(\theta \mid\left\{y_{1}, \ldots, y_{n}\right\}\right) \asymp e^{-n \mathcal{I}(\theta)} \tag{48}
\]
where \(\mathcal{I}(\theta)\) acts as an effective rate function combining prior and likelihood contributions.

title: 13  The Typical Animals Encountered in the Statistical Zoo
text:
13.4 Probabilistic Inequalities in Bayesian Inference
Several classical inequalities in probability theory play a central role in Bayesian inference. Beyond providing non-asymptotic bounds, they underpin variational approximations, posterior concentration results, and Bayesian optimality principles. We review Markov’s, Chebyshev’s, and Jensen’s inequalities, emphasizing their relevance for Bayesian reasoning.

13.4.1 Markov’s Inequality and Tail Bounds
Let \(X\) be a nonnegative random variable, \(X \geq 0\), with finite expectation \(\mathbb{E}[X]<\infty\). Then, for any \(a>0\),
\[
\mathbb{P}(X \geq a) \leq \frac{\mathbb{E}[X]}{a} \tag{49}
\]
Proof. Since \(X / a \geq \mathbf{1}_{X \geq a}\),
\[
\mathbb{E}[X] \geq \mathbb{E}\left[X \mathbf{1}_{X \geq a}\right] \geq a \mathbb{P}(X \geq a) \tag{50}
\]
Markov’s inequality provides a distribution-free upper bound on tail probabilities using only the first moment. The bound is typically loose, but its generality makes it a fundamental tool.
Bayesian relevance. In Bayesian inference, Markov’s inequality is often used to control posterior tails of nonnegative quantities. For a posterior distribution \(\pi(\theta \mid y)\) and a nonnegative loss function \(L(\theta)\),
\[
\mathbb{P}_{\pi(\theta \mid y)}(L(\theta) \geq a) \leq \frac{\mathbb{E}_{\pi(\theta \mid y)}[L(\theta)]}{a} \tag{51}
\]
Applying Markov’s inequality to \(e^{t X}\) yields exponential (Chernoff-type) bounds, which form the basis of many Bayesian concentration results.


13.4.2 Chebyshev’s Inequality and Posterior Concentration
Let \(X\) be a random variable with mean \(\mu=\mathbb{E}[X]\) and finite variance \(\sigma^{2}=\operatorname{Var}(X)\). For any \(k>0\),
\[
\mathbb{P}(|X-\mu| \geq k) \leq \frac{\sigma^{2}}{k^{2}} \tag{52}
\]
Derivation. (52) follows directly from Markov’s inequality applied to the nonnegative random variable \((X-\mu)^{2}\).
Chebyshev’s inequality bounds the probability of large deviations from the mean using only second-moment information. It provides a minimal, assumption-free form of concentration.
Bayesian relevance. For a posterior distribution \(\pi(\theta \mid y)\),
\[
\mathbb{P}(|\theta-\mathbb{E}[\theta \mid y]| \geq k \mid y) \leq \frac{\operatorname{Var}(\theta \mid y)}{k^{2}} \tag{53}
\]
As posterior variances shrink (e.g. in large-sample limits), Chebyshev’s inequality guarantees posterior concentration in probability, independently of distributional details.


13.4.3 Jensen’s Inequality and Variational Structure
Let \(X\) be a random variable and let \(\varphi\) be a convex (such as the \(\exp\)). Then
\[
\varphi(\mathbb{E}[X]) \leq \mathbb{E}[\varphi(X)] \tag{54}
\]
If \(\varphi\) is concave (such as the \(\log\)), the inequality is reversed.
Role in Bayesian inference. For observed data \(y\) and latent variables \(\theta\),
\[
\begin{align*}
\log p(y) & =\log \int p(y, \theta) d \theta \\
& =\log \mathbb{E}_{q(\theta)}\left[\frac{p(y, \theta)}{q(\theta)}\right] \\
& \geq \mathbb{E}_{q(\theta)}\left[\log \frac{p(y, \theta)}{q(\theta)}\right] \tag{55-57}
\end{align*}
\]
This defines the evidence lower bound (ELBO), whose maximization is the basis of variational Bayesian inference.
Finally, note that the nonnegativity of the Kullback-Leibler divergence,
\[
\mathrm{KL}(q \| p) \equiv \mathbb{E}_{q}\left[\log \frac{q}{p}\right] \geq 0 \tag{58}
\]
is a direct consequence of Jensen’s inequality and underlies Bayesian optimality and consistency results.

title: 13  The Typical Animals Encountered in the Statistical Zoo
text:
The sequence \(X_{n}\) is said to converge in probability to \(X\), written \(X_{n} \xrightarrow{\mathbb{P}} X\), if for every \(\varepsilon>0\), \[\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} \mathbb{P}\left(\left|X_{n}-X\right|>\varepsilon\right)=0 \tag{40}\]↩︎
The sequence \(X_{n}\) is said to converge almost surely (a.s.) to \(X\), written \(X_{n} \xrightarrow{\text { a.s. }} X\), if \[\mathbb{P}\left(\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} X_{n}=X\right)=1 \tag{42}\] Equivalently, \[\mathbb{P}\left(\left\{\omega \in \Omega: X_{n}(\omega) \rightarrow X(\omega)\right\}\right)=1 \tag{43}\]↩︎
We write \(\bar{X}_{n} \approx x\) to mean that \(\bar{X}_{n}\) takes value in a neighborhood of \(x\) since \(\bar{X}_{n}\) is a continuous random variable for \(n \rightarrow \infty\).↩︎
